content.text: is an open-source LLM application development platform that lets you build chatbots, agents, RAG pipelines, and multi-node AI workflows without writing infrastructure code. You configure apps visually in the Studio, publish them as REST APIs, and optionally embed them directly in web pages or chat platforms.

attrs.alt: Dify platform architecture: App Types → Core Platform → REST API → Integrations

content.text: Dify ships as a Docker Compose stack. Self-hosting takes about five minutes; Dify Cloud is available if you prefer not to manage infrastructure.

# Clone and start Dify with Docker Compose
git clone https://github.com/langgenius/dify.git
cd dify/docker
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env to set SECRET_KEY and optional SMTP/storage settings
docker compose up -d

# Dify is now at http://localhost/
# First run: create admin account via the web UI

# For production, also configure:
#   STORAGE_TYPE=s3  (or azure/gcs)
#   DB_* for external Postgres
#   REDIS_* for external Redis

# Dify Cloud — no install needed
# 1. Sign up at https://dify.ai
# 2. Create a workspace
# 3. Add model providers under Settings → Model Provider
#    (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure OpenAI, Google, local Ollama, etc.)
# 4. Create an app from the Studio tab
to a 32-character random string before starting Docker Compose. An empty or default key is a security vulnerability in production.
the top-level unit in Dify. Each app has a type (Chatbot, Text Generator, Agent, Workflow, or Chatflow) and exposes its own API key and endpoint.
a configured LLM backend (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Ollama, etc.). One workspace can have many providers; each app selects which model it uses.
the visual prompt editor where you set the system prompt, add variables (
), and test the app interactively before deploying.
a collection of documents chunked, embedded, and indexed for retrieval. Attach a Knowledge Base to any app to enable RAG.
a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of nodes — LLM, Code, HTTP Request, Condition, Iteration, Tool. Workflows can be triggered by an API or embedded inside a Chatflow app.
an external capability injected into an Agent or Workflow. Built-in tools include web search, calculator, Wikipedia, and DALL-E; custom tools are defined via OpenAPI 3.0 schemas.
. Pass the user query, any input variables, and optionally a

import requests

BASE_URL = "https://api.dify.ai/v1"  # or http://localhost/v1 for self-hosted
API_KEY  = "app-xxxxxxxxxxxx"         # from App → API Access

response = requests.post(
    f"{BASE_URL}/chat-messages",
    headers={
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    },
    json={
        "query": "What are the benefits of vector databases?",
        "inputs": {},                  # named input variables defined in the prompt
        "response_mode": "blocking",   # or "streaming"
        "user": "user-42",             # caller-supplied user identifier
        "conversation_id": "",         # empty to start a new conversation
    },
)
data = response.json()
print(data["answer"])
print("conversation_id:", data["conversation_id"])  # reuse for follow-up turns
returned in the first response must be echoed back in subsequent requests to maintain conversation history. Dify manages memory server-side — no client-side message buffering is needed.
signals completion and carries usage statistics.

import httpx, json

API_KEY = "app-xxxxxxxxxxxx"

async def stream_chat(question: str, conversation_id: str = ""):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with client.stream(
            "POST",
            "https://api.dify.ai/v1/chat-messages",
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}",
                "Content-Type": "application/json",
            },
            json={
                "query": question,
                "inputs": {},
                "response_mode": "streaming",
                "user": "user-42",
                "conversation_id": conversation_id,
            },
            timeout=120,
        ) as resp:
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data:"):
                    payload = json.loads(line[5:].strip())
                    event = payload.get("event")
                    if event == "message":
                        print(payload["answer"], end="", flush=True)
                    elif event == "message_end":
                        print()
                        return payload.get("conversation_id", "")

import asyncio
cid = asyncio.run(stream_chat("Explain vector quantisation step by step."))
# Reuse cid in the next call to continue the conversation
. Define named variables in the Prompt IDE (e.g.,
) and supply their values in the
dict maps to the Start node's input variables. The response includes the outputs of every terminal node.

# Workflow App — trigger a multi-node DAG
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "https://api.dify.ai/v1/workflows/run",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer app-xxxxxxxxxxxx"},
    json={
        "inputs": {
            "document_url": "https://example.com/report.pdf",
            "language":     "Spanish",
        },
        "response_mode": "blocking",
        "user": "translator-bot",
    },
)
data = response.json()
# data["data"]["outputs"] contains the final node outputs keyed by variable name
print(data["data"]["outputs"])
on Workflow apps too — long DAG chains benefit greatly from incremental delivery, especially those with LLM nodes early in the graph that feed into downstream processing.
call. Supported types include PDF, DOCX, images (PNG/JPG), and audio.

import requests

API_KEY = "app-xxxxxxxxxxxx"

# 1. Upload the file
with open("report.pdf", "rb") as f:
    upload_resp = requests.post(
        "https://api.dify.ai/v1/files/upload",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"},
        files={"file": ("report.pdf", f, "application/pdf")},
        data={"user": "analyst-1"},
    )
file_id = upload_resp.json()["id"]

# 2. Reference the uploaded file in a chat message
chat_resp = requests.post(
    "https://api.dify.ai/v1/chat-messages",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "query": "Summarise the key findings in this report.",
        "inputs": {},
        "response_mode": "blocking",
        "user": "analyst-1",
        "files": [
            {
                "type": "document",
                "transfer_method": "local_file",
                "upload_file_id": file_id,
            }
        ],
    },
)
print(chat_resp.json()["answer"])
Dify's Knowledge API lets you programmatically create datasets, upload documents, and query retrieval results. This is useful for CI pipelines that sync documentation into Dify on deploy.

import requests

BASE_URL = "https://api.dify.ai/v1"
DATASET_API_KEY = "dataset-xxxxxxxxxxxx"  # from Knowledge → API

# Create a dataset
ds = requests.post(
    f"{BASE_URL}/datasets",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {DATASET_API_KEY}"},
    json={"name": "Product Docs Q3"},
).json()
dataset_id = ds["id"]

# Upload a document into the dataset
with open("faq.txt", "rb") as f:
    doc_resp = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/datasets/{dataset_id}/document/create-by-file",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {DATASET_API_KEY}"},
        files={"file": ("faq.txt", f, "text/plain")},
        data={"data": '{"indexing_technique":"high_quality","process_rule":{"mode":"automatic"}}'},
    )
print("Indexing status:", doc_resp.json()["document"]["display_status"])
semantic similarity, full-text search, or hybrid. Set per-dataset under Knowledge → Retrieval Settings.
custom splitter, overlap, max chunk length. Smaller chunks improve precision; larger chunks preserve context.

Agent apps use a ReAct or Function Calling strategy to plan which tools to invoke. You configure the agent's model, system prompt, tool list, and iteration limit in the Dify UI. No code is needed to set up basic agents.
DuckDuckGo search, Wikipedia, calculator, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, code interpreter.
define via OpenAPI 3.0 schema under Tools → Custom Tools. Dify makes the HTTP call and returns the response to the LLM.
default 5. Increase for complex multi-step tasks, but set a ceiling to prevent runaway loops.
Collect thumbs-up / thumbs-down feedback per message via the Feedbacks endpoint. Annotations appear in the app's Logs panel and can be used to curate a fine-tuning dataset.

import requests

# Submit feedback (👍 / 👎) on a message
response = requests.post(
    f"https://api.dify.ai/v1/messages/{message_id}/feedbacks",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer app-xxxxxxxxxxxx"},
    json={
        "rating": "like",         # or "dislike" or null to clear
        "user": "user-42",
        "content": "Great answer!",
    },
)
print(response.json())
# Feedback is visible in the app's Annotation panel for RLHF-style correction
Embedding the App as a Web Widget
Every Dify app can be embedded into any HTML page with a single script tag. Copy the snippet from App → Overview → Embed.

<!-- Embed Dify chat widget -->
<script
  src="https://udify.app/embed.min.js"
  id="YOUR_APP_ID"
  defer>
</script>
<!-- The widget appears as a chat bubble in the bottom-right corner.
     Optionally configure position, theme colour, and default greeting
     in the app's Embed Settings. -->
with your own domain. The embed widget handles streaming, file upload, and multi-turn memory without any additional JavaScript.
Dify supports per-app environment variables set in the Prompt IDE under
. Secret variables are stored encrypted and never exposed in API responses or logs.
per-user API keys, tenant identifiers, compliance metadata passed at call time via
stored in the workspace's Model Provider config, not in individual apps — rotate keys in one place.
or Kubernetes Secrets for infrastructure-level credentials (Postgres, Redis, S3).
Dify vs Flowise vs Langflow vs n8n
across users exposes one user's history to another. Generate one UUID per user session and store it server-side, never in the browser URL.
may contain multi-token chunks, not single characters. Parse the JSON on every
line rather than concatenating raw bytes.
newly uploaded documents are not immediately searchable. Poll the document status endpoint until
descriptions cause the LLM to mis-select tools. Write the OpenAPI
as a single precise sentence describing exactly when the tool should be called.
Dify's stack (API, Worker, Web, Postgres, Redis, Weaviate) requires at least 4 GB RAM. On Docker Desktop, raise the memory limit under Settings → Resources before starting the stack.
each model provider in Dify can have a separate API key and rate limit. Use the workspace-level fallback model setting to route to a cheaper provider when the primary hits its limit.
Dify is an open-source LLM platform with five app types (Chatbot, Text Generator, Agent, Workflow, Chatflow) — each published as an independent REST API.
Attach a Knowledge Base to any app for RAG retrieval; manage documents programmatically via the Dataset API.
Agent apps use built-in tools (search, calculator, code interpreter) or custom OpenAPI-defined HTTP tools without writing LLM orchestration code.
Embed any app in a web page with a one-line script tag copied from the app's Embed Settings.
Self-host with Docker Compose (≥4 GB RAM) or use Dify Cloud; rotate model credentials centrally under workspace Model Provider settings.
